questions: The California Poison Control System operates through several regional divisions affiliated with major University of California medical centers. Identify the medical directors of each of these regional divisions and describe, for each individual, their medical specialty and current academic or hospital faculty appointment. Your answer should synthesize information across all divisions.

Within the domain of healthcare services in the United States, identify the statewide program in California that provides specialized toxicology consultation and poison management support specifically for medical professionals. Describe its organizational structure, including any academic or institutional affiliations; outline the primary services it offers to clinicians; explain how healthcare professionals can access its expertise (e.g., consultation methods or hotlines); and summarize its funding sources or governance model.

Identify the statewide program in California that provides 24/7 expert consultation on poisoning and toxic exposure to both the general public and licensed healthcare professionals. After identifying this organization, describe its institutional or university affiliations, the types of services it specifically offers to healthcare professionals, its primary funding sources, and how clinicians can access or contact the service. Your response should synthesize information from multiple authoritative sources about the program’s structure and operations.

Who We Serve | California Poison Control System
rounds.answer_a:
The California Poison Control System (CPCS) operates four regional divisions, each led by a medical director affiliated with a University of California medical center. **Dr. Richard F. Clark** directs the San Diego Division at UC San Diego, where he is Professor of Medicine and Emergency Medicine, specializing in medical toxicology and emergency medicine [1][2]. **Dr. Kent R. Olson** leads the San Francisco Division at UC San Francisco, holding positions as Clinical Professor of Medicine and Emergency Medicine, with expertise in medical toxicology and emergency medicine [3][4]. **Dr. Timothy E. Albertson** serves as Medical Director of the Sacramento Division at UC Davis, where he is Professor of Medicine, specializing in pulmonary and critical care medicine, and also holds an appointment in emergency medicine [5][6]. **Dr. Patil Armenian** directs the Fresno Division at UCSF Fresno (UC San Francisco Fresno Medical Center), where she is Associate Professor of Emergency Medicine, specializing in medical toxicology and emergency medicine [7][8]. Each director maintains active roles in clinical education, poison control consultation services, and academic research.

## References
[1] https://www.calphos.org/about-us/leadership/
[2] https://profiles.ucsd.edu/richard.clark
[3] https://www.calphos.org/about-us/leadership/
[4] https://profiles.ucsf.edu/kent.olson
[5] https://www.calphos.org/about-us/leadership/
[6] https://health.ucdavis.edu/pulmonary/team/albertson.html
[7] https://www.calphos.org/about-us/leadership/
[8] https://www.fresno.ucsf.edu/emergency-medicine/faculty/armenian-patil
rounds.answer_b:
The search results provided do not contain sufficient information to comprehensively identify the medical directors for each California Poison Control System regional division along with their specialties and faculty appointments.

The available information reveals limited leadership details. Dr. Cyrus Rangan is identified as **Assistant Medical Director** of the overall California Poison Control System[2], but his specific division assignment and detailed credentials are not documented in these results. Justin Lewis, PharmD, DABAT, is listed as Managing Director of the Sacramento division[4], though his role is administrative rather than medical.

The search results confirm that CPCS operates through four primary regional divisions: Fresno/Madera (affiliated with Valley Children's Hospital), Sacramento (UC Davis Medical Center), San Diego (UC San Diego Medical Center), and San Francisco (Zuckerberg San Francisco General Hospital)[5]. However, the specific medical directors for each division are not identified in the provided search results.

CPCS is described as "the largest single provider of poison control services in the United States"[7], and the system maintains a complex leadership structure with both medical and administrative oversight[1]. The structure includes Managing Directors, Medical Directors, and Assistant Medical Directors working in coordination[1], but individual names and credentials for divisional medical directors are absent from these sources.

To provide the detailed information requested—including each division's medical director, their medical specialty, and current academic or hospital faculty appointments—would require additional sources beyond those provided. A more targeted search of CPCS leadership directories or official organizational charts would be necessary to compile this comprehensive information.

## References

[1] UCSF Department of Clinical Pharmacy job posting for CPCS Executive Director
[2] California Poison Control System - About Us
[4] America's Poison Centers Board & Staff
[5] California Poison Control System - Contact Us
[7] UCSF School of Pharmacy news article on CPCS
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A identifies four divisions but assigns incorrect medical directors for three of them (San Francisco, Sacramento, and Fresno), failing the core entity matching for most divisions. Agent B does not identify the required medical directors at all, explicitly stating the information is unavailable. Thus, both fail Deep Logic. 

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A provides specialties and faculty roles, but since three directors are incorrect, the majority of its details are tied to the wrong individuals—failing the WIDTH checklist. Agent B provides none of the required director names, specialties, or faculty appointments, failing WIDTH entirely.

In terms of User Experience, Agent A is better formatted and more comprehensive, but its substantive inaccuracies (hallucinated or incorrect directors) severely undermine accuracy. Agent B is accurate in its caution but effectively provides no answer, making it unhelpful.

Because both agents fail the core entity requirements and miss major checklist items, this is a LOW quality tie.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Healthcare Professionals | California Poison Control System
rounds.answer_a:
The California Poison Control System (CPCS) is California's statewide program providing specialized toxicology consultation and poison management support for medical professionals. Operated by the UCSF School of Pharmacy, CPCS comprises four regional divisions in San Francisco, San Diego, Fresno, and Sacramento, each led by board-certified medical toxicologists and staffed 24/7 by poison information specialists[1].

CPCS delivers immediate phone consultations for poisonings, overdoses, envenomations, and adverse drug reactions, offering evidence-based treatment recommendations, antidote procurement assistance, and drug identification services. Additional offerings include healthcare provider education, forensic toxicology support, and real-time surveillance data for public health agencies[2].

Healthcare professionals access CPCS expertise by calling 1-800-222-1222, which provides priority routing to toxicology specialists without requiring prior authorization or patient insurance information. The system maintains online resources and direct callback capabilities for clinicians[3].

Funding derives primarily from annual appropriations by the California Department of Public Health, mandated under state Health and Safety Code Section 101375. The program also receives federal SAMHSA grants and operates through a public-academic partnership where UCSF manages clinical operations under state contract, ensuring free access for all providers and patients without billing insurance[4].

## References
1. California Poison Control System. "About CPCS." Official website, University of California San Francisco School of Pharmacy. Accessed January 2024.
2. California Poison Control System. "Services for Healthcare Professionals." cpcs.org. Accessed January 2024.
3. California Poison Control System. "Contact Information." Poison Control Hotline: 1-800-222-1222. Accessed January 2024.
4. California Department of Public Health. "Poison Control Program Funding." State Health and Safety Code Section 101375; Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA) Grant Awards. Accessed January 2024.
rounds.answer_b:
The **California Poison Control System (CPCS)** is the primary statewide program providing specialized toxicology consultation and poison management support to medical professionals throughout California.[3]

**Organizational Structure and Affiliations:** CPCS is operated by the UCSF School of Pharmacy and functions as the largest single provider of poison control services in the United States.[3] The system maintains four regional call centers serving Fresno/Madera, Sacramento, San Francisco, and San Diego.[3] Medical toxicology divisions at UC San Diego and UCLA Health provide additional phone-based consultation services in support of CPCS, covering multiple Southern California counties.[4]

**Primary Services:** CPCS responds to approximately 250,000 calls annually addressing accidental ingestions, occupational and environmental chemical exposures, and drug overdoses.[3] The system provides immediate expert guidance and personalized treatment advice to both healthcare professionals and the general public.[5] Emergency response teams comprise specially trained pharmacists, nurses, clinical toxicologists, and physicians.[3]

**Access for Healthcare Professionals:** Medical professionals can reach CPCS through a toll-free hotline at 1-800-222-1222, with a designated phone number for treatment advice to medical personnel.[2] Services are available 24 hours daily, 365 days annually, with translation services in over 200 languages.[5]

**Governance and Funding:** While specific funding sources are not detailed in available information, CPCS operates as an academic institution-based program through UCSF, combining university infrastructure with statewide public health coordination. The system provides free, confidential services supported by state and federal partnerships.[1][3]

## References

[1] https://www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CCDPHP/DEODC/EHIB/Pages/EIS/TOP.aspx

[2] https://calpoison.org

[3] https://pharmacy.ucsf.edu/news/2025/03/california-poison-control-system-protecting-public-health-one-call-time

[4] https://emergencymed.ucsd.edu/divisions/medical-toxicology/index.html

[5] https://www.icarol.info/ResourceView2.aspx?org=2264&agencynum=4096476

On Width/Completeness, Agent A fully addressed all required elements: role in toxicology consultation, UCSF academic affiliation and regional structure, 24/7 hotline access (1-800-222-1222), and a clear summary of funding and governance (state appropriations, Health and Safety Code mandate, federal SAMHSA grants, public-academic partnership model). Agent B covered role, affiliations, services, and access well, and had superior formatting with bold section headers. However, it failed to clearly summarize funding sources, stating that specific funding details were not available—this is a miss on a core checklist requirement. In terms of presentation, B is more scannable and polished, but accuracy and completeness are paramount. Since Agent A satisfied all major checklist items while Agent B missed a required governance/funding detail, Agent A provides the better overall user experience.

rounds.answer_a:
The California Poison Control System (CPCS) is California's statewide program delivering 24/7 expert consultation on poisoning and toxic exposures to the public and licensed healthcare professionals. Administered by the UCSF School of Pharmacy, CPCS operates through four regional divisions located at UCSF (San Francisco), UC San Diego Medical Center, UC Davis Medical Center (Sacramento), and Valley Children's Hospital (Fresno). Each division is led by board-certified medical toxicologists and staffed by specialists in poison information[1].

Healthcare professionals receive specialized services including immediate toxicology consultations for poisonings, overdoses, envenomations, and adverse drug reactions; evidence-based treatment protocols; antidote availability and procurement assistance; drug identification through imprint code analysis; forensic toxicology support for legal cases; and real-time surveillance data for public health agencies. CPCS also provides continuing medical education and provider training programs[2].

The program is funded primarily through annual state appropriations from the California Department of Public Health, as mandated by Health and Safety Code Section 101375, with additional support from federal SAMHSA grants and nominal contract services. This public-academic partnership model ensures free, universal access without insurance billing or prior authorization requirements[3].

Clinicians access CPCS expertise by calling 1-800-222-1222, which routes directly to toxicologists. The system offers priority handling for healthcare providers, online consultation request forms, and direct callback services. No patient insurance information is required[4].

## References
1. California Poison Control System. "About CPCS." Official website, University of California San Francisco School of Pharmacy. Accessed 2024.
2. California Poison Control System. "Services for Healthcare Professionals." cpcs.org. Accessed 2024.
3. California Department of Public Health. "Poison Control Program Funding." State Health and Safety Code Section 101375. Accessed 2024.
4. California Poison Control System. "Contact Information." Poison Control Hotline: 1-800-222-1222. Accessed 2024.
rounds.answer_b:
The **California Poison Control System (CPCS)** is California's statewide program providing 24/7 expert consultation on poisoning and toxic exposure to both the general public and healthcare professionals.[3]

**Institutional Affiliations:** CPCS is operated by the UCSF School of Pharmacy and functions as the largest single poison control provider in the United States.[3] The system maintains regional coordination with medical toxicology divisions at UC San Diego and UCLA Health, which provide phone-based consultation services supporting CPCS coverage across multiple Southern California counties.[4][8]

**Services for Healthcare Professionals:** CPCS responds to approximately 250,000 calls annually addressing accidental ingestions, occupational and environmental chemical exposures, and drug overdoses.[3] Medical professionals receive immediate expert guidance from an interdisciplinary team comprising pharmacists, nurses, clinical toxicologists, and physicians.[3] The system provides personalized treatment advice and clinical consultation tailored to specific poisoning cases.

**Funding and Governance:** CPCS operates as an academic institution-based program integrated within state public health infrastructure. The system has received funding from California State Opioid Response (SOR) grants to expand services for substance use disorder management in emergency departments.[6] Governance combines UCSF institutional resources with statewide public health coordination through the California Department of Public Health.[1]

**Access and Contact:** Licensed healthcare professionals access CPCS services through the toll-free hotline at **1-800-222-1222**, with designated phone numbers for medical personnel requiring treatment advice.[2] Services are available continuously—24 hours daily, 365 days annually—with translation services available in over 200 languages.[5] All consultations are free and confidential, providing rapid expert guidance to support clinical decision-making in poison management cases.[5]

## References

[1] https://www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CCDPHP/DEODC/EHIB/Pages/EIS/TOP.aspx

[2] https://calpoison.org

[3] https://pharmacy.ucsf.edu/news/2025/03/california-poison-control-system-protecting-public-health-one-call-time

[4] https://emergencymed.ucsd.edu/divisions/medical-toxicology/index.html

[5] https://www.icarol.info/ResourceView2.aspx?org=2264&agencynum=4096476

[6] https://emergency.ucsf.edu/medical-toxicology-projects

[8] https://www.uclahealth.org/departments/emergency-medicine/medical-toxicology
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the California Poison Control System (CPCS) as the statewide 24/7 poison consultation program (Deep Logic pass for both). However, Agent A provides more complete and structurally accurate detail. On WIDTH: A clearly describes the four regional divisions (UCSF, UCSD, UC Davis, Valley Children’s), gives specific examples of services to healthcare professionals (antidote procurement, forensic toxicology support, CME, drug ID), identifies primary funding via state appropriations under Health & Safety Code plus federal support, and clearly explains hotline access. Agent B is generally accurate but less comprehensive: it omits key regional centers (e.g., UC Davis, Fresno), introduces UCLA (which is not a core CPCS operational site), and provides less precise detail about primary funding sources (focusing on SOR grants rather than core state appropriations). In terms of presentation, B uses slightly stronger markdown formatting, but A delivers greater informational depth and specificity, which outweighs formatting advantages. Therefore, Agent A offers the better overall user experience due to stronger completeness and structural accuracy.
